newText: Tomatoes only reached Spain in numbers in the 1600s. So the modern dish is newer than the idea behind it. Today it shows the easy style of Spanish home cooking. Three things, one minute, a plate worth slowing down for.

Paneer tikka masala is the curry-house main for people who want tikka masala without the chicken. It sits on the menu next to saag paneer. Both are veggie mains, but they pull in different ways. Saag paneer leans on spinach and cream. This one leans on a rich, spiced sauce and the smoke from charred paneer. The char carries into the sauce in a way that plain fried paneer does not.

Pappa al pomodoro started in Florence and spread across Tuscany. The 19th-century cookery writer Artusi set it down as a workman's lunch. Stale bread got a second life in summer tomatoes and olive oil. The dish went national in the late 1950s after a children's TV show featured a song about it. A peasant dish turned into a comfort-food classic. In Florence it still sits on the most traditional trattoria menus next to ribollita and trippa alla fiorentina. It is part of the Florentine cucina povera, now served with pride because it was once thought too humble for a menu.

The Emilian version of porcini pasta uses cream. The Tuscan version uses olive oil and the dark soaking liquid from the dried mushrooms. The result is sharper, darker, and more intense. No dairy gets in the way of the porcini. The soaking liquid cooks down to almost nothing. It leaves a dark coat on the mushrooms. Pasta water then loosens it into a sauce.

Pappardelle is wide enough to hold the sauce. It is sturdy enough to stand up to the strong mushroom flavour. Fresh pappardelle, if you can find it, cooks in 2 to 3 minutes. The texture is softer. Dried pappardelle takes a little longer. It stands up to tossing without breaking.

Porcini pasta in olive oil is the older Tuscan version. It belongs to the hill towns south of Florence, where the forests yield porcini from late summer. The cream version is from Emilia and came later. It is now better known outside Italy. The Tuscan food writer Giuliano Bugialli marks the olive oil version as the original. He notes that the cream was a postwar addition.

Wild boar numbers in Tuscany have grown a lot since the 1970s. Hunting cinghiale is part of the autumn social calendar in villages across the region. The ragu from the hunt is slow-cooked with the local red wine, usually Chianti. It is served over pappardelle, the wide pasta that holds a thick meat sauce without breaking. Restaurants across Siena, Arezzo, and Grosseto serve it from October to February.
Tear the mozzarella and drain it on kitchen paper for 10 minutes before use. Buffalo mozzarella holds more water than cow mozzarella, so let it sit a little longer.

Sicily and Campania both claim parmigiana di melanzane. The name comes from the way the slices are layered. It is not from Parmesan or from Parma. The Sicilian word parmiciana means a row of overlapping slats, like shutters. The aubergine slices stack the same way. It is a summer dish, made when aubergines are at their peak. People eat it at room temperature as often as hot.
Cook the tagliatelle in salted boiling water, following the packet timing. Drain it. Keep back a little of the cooking water.
Drain the pasta straight into the sauce pan. Or add a ladle of pasta water just after draining. That stops it sticking.

Aglio e olio is the midnight meal of Naples. It is what you make when you come home late and find pasta, garlic, and a bottle of oil. Its place in Italian cooking is larger than the short ingredient list suggests. It shows that good cooking does not need many things. It needs good oil and good method. Cheese on toast and beans on toast play the same role in Britain. Both kinds of dish say as much about a food culture as any fancier plate.

newContent.text: Pastitsio is the baked pasta dish at the heart of Greek home cooking. It is deeper and more aromatic than its Italian cousins. Cinnamon runs through the lamb sauce. A thick, egg-rich
sets almost like a custard on top. The result holds together in clean slices. It travels well. And it is better the next day, once the layers have had time to settle.

newText: Patatas bravas is the tapas dish of Madrid. It is the one that tells you whether a bar is for locals or for tourists. The local version has a proper sauce with heat and depth. The tourist version is often just ketchup with a smear of mayonnaise. The dish appeared in Madrid's tabernas around the mid-1900s. It has since become one of the most copied and most often badly made dishes in the Spanish canon. The play of textures makes it work: crunchy potato, thick spiced sauce, cold creamy aioli.

Smoked aubergine shows up across the Eastern Mediterranean and the Levant. In Turkey it is patlıcan salatası. In Lebanon and Syria it is baba ghanoush or mutabbal. In Georgia it is badrijani. In Greece it is melitzanosalata. All come from the same idea: the spongy flesh of an aubergine becomes something special when held over a live flame. It soaks up the smoke from its own burning skin. The Turkish version tends to be lighter than the Lebanese. It uses less tahini, or none, and sometimes a spoonful of yoghurt instead. It is a meze constant, found at every table before the main course. It also works on its own with flatbread.
Gently melt the rest of the peanut butter, chocolate, and all the sugar in a pan. Stir now and then. Stop when the sugar has just about melted.
Put the no-stir peanut butter, honey, rolled oats, vanilla protein powder, salt, and mini dark chocolate chips into a mixing bowl. Use a rubber spatula to mix. Stop when the batter holds together.

newContent.text: is more forgiving than a rice risotto. The barley takes longer to overcook. It soaks up stock over 40 to 45 minutes with only the odd stir. A rice risotto needs constant work; this one does not. Dried porcini soaked in hot water give a deep, earthy base note. The soaking liquid makes good stock too.

newText: Poached pears in wine are one of the oldest European desserts. The combination shows up in medieval English and French manuscripts. Wine, sugar, and spice turn raw fruit into a table piece. The same idea ran through the Tudor and Stuart years. By the late 1800s, poached pears were a standard household dessert in Britain. Serve them cold with the syrup. They work as a dinner-party starter alongside a sharp cheese. They also work as a pudding with cream.
sourceNotesAppend: Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) places poached pears in the autumn dessert chapter, a fair marker of how standard the dish had become in Victorian British home cooking.

newText: Beef pelau takes the same approach as chicken pelau but needs more time. The beef wants a 30-minute head start before the rice goes in. Otherwise the meat will still be tough when the rice is done. The caramel-browning step is the same and just as key. Take the sugar to a dark, smoking amber before the beef touches it.

Beef pelau is less common than chicken pelau in everyday Trinidadian cooking. It shows up at large gatherings where the scale of catering calls for something more substantial. The beef version is slower, heavier, and has a deeper colour from the longer browning. Served with macaroni pie and callaloo at a party, it covers the full spread of Trinidadian one-pot cooking. The caramel technique comes from West Africa. The bean and rice combo comes from across the Caribbean. The coconut and squash base belongs to the islands.

Pelau is Sunday food and party food across Trinidad. It sits at the centre of the table at any big gathering: a christening, a birthday, a cricket day, alongside macaroni pie and callaloo. The dish pulls Trinidad's food history into one pot. The caramel-browning method comes from West Africa. The rice and peas idea comes from across the Caribbean. The pigeon peas came up from South America. The coconut and allspice belong to the island. It is the most Trinidadian dish there is.

Pelmeni come from Siberia. They sit in the wider Ural-Altaic family of filled dumplings, alongside Chinese jiaozi, Mongolian buuz, and Georgian khinkali. In Siberia they were made at the start of winter and frozen outdoors in their thousands. They were fast food through the cold months. Today they are eaten across Russia and sold in every supermarket freezer. The homemade version, eaten fresh with butter, is another thing entirely.

The quattro formaggi combination is not fixed. Different regions and different cooks pick different cheeses. The balance is what matters: sharp with mild, hard with soft, melting with flavour-giving. The version here uses gorgonzola for a strong blue note. Parmesan adds depth and salt. Fontina gives a smooth melt, not stringy. Mozzarella adds stretch and a mild creaminess. Cream loosens the sauce and gives it body.

Pasta ai quattro formaggi shows up all over Italy. It does not belong to one region. It is a method more than a local dish. The mix of four cheeses shifts as you move around the country. In the north, fontina and grana padano lead. In the south, pecorino and mozzarella lead. Italian restaurants outside Italy made it a menu fixture from the 1990s. It sits between lighter tomato sauces and heavier meat dishes. It works for people who like cream sauces and mild blue cheese.

Penne al salmone belongs to the 1980s wave of Italian cucina moderna. It is quicker and lighter than the classics. It uses ingredients that would have been unusual a decade before, when supermarkets started to grow. Smoked salmon used to be costly and rare in Italy. By the mid-1980s it was standard in northern Italian home cooking. The sauce is simple. Soften shallot, flame off a splash of vodka, reduce cream briefly. Slip in the salmon strips at the very end so they warm through without cooking.

Penne al salmone is northern Italian city cooking from the 1980s. That was the decade when Italian food became both more worldly and more about convenience. It turned up on Italian restaurant menus in Britain from the mid-1990s. It was a reliable crowd-pleaser: clearly Italian, mild in flavour, and built from things you can keep in the fridge for a week.

Boscaiola belongs to the wooded interior of central Italy. The region covers Tuscany, Umbria, and the Apennine foothills. Foraging for fungi is a serious autumn pursuit there. Pork is cured and eaten in every form. The dish does not appear in coastal or southern Italian cooking, which tends to be lighter. In Umbria, where black truffle grows in plenty, boscaiola sometimes carries shaved tartufo nero on top. Elsewhere it stays simple.

Penne alla vodka sits in an odd spot. It is an Italian dish that became more famous in New York than in Italy. Roman restaurants began serving it in the 1970s. Manhattan Italian-American restaurants made it a fixture in the 1980s. It went out of style for a while, then came back as a comfort-food classic. The dish lasts partly because it is fast, under 30 minutes from start to plate. It also lasts because the vodka trick really works. The sauce tastes of more than its ingredients.
Add the diced potatoes and red pepper. Keep cooking and stirring now and then until the potato is soft, about 10 minutes.
Vegetarian: swap the meat for roasted summer veg, like courgette, pepper, and aubergine, with crumbled feta.

Pepper sauce is the condiment on every Trinidadian table. It goes on doubles, stew chicken, macaroni pie, roti, and almost anything else. The Trinidadian style uses mustard, turmeric, and vinegar. That is different from the Bajan style (scotch bonnet in vinegar, very plain) and from the Jamaican scotch bonnet sauces. Brands like Matouk's and Chief's set the standard. Home versions vary more, and lean either much milder or much hotter than the bottled ones, depending on the family.

Pepperpot is not a dish most people outside the Caribbean know well. That is a shame, because it deserves a wider audience. This is the Jamaican version: a thick, dark, meaty broth. The flavour comes from allspice, scotch bonnet, and long cooking. It does not use the cassava-based cassareep that goes into the Guyanese version. That is a different dish. The Jamaican one is simpler, more herb-forward, and very good on a cold evening.

Peshwari naan has a clear place on a British curry-house menu. It is the sweet bread. People order it when they want a contrast to the savoury mains, not just a plain side. The sweetness works alongside mild curries like korma, passanda, and butter chicken. It also balances a table where the main dishes lean hot. The scattering of flaked almonds on the surface has become a visual shorthand for the dish across British Indian menus.

Cuban picadillo has a flavour mix that sounds unlikely until you try it. Ground beef goes in the pan with olives, capers, and raisins. The sweetness of the raisins, the brine of the olives and capers, the earthiness of cumin: these come together in a way that has roots in Spanish colonial cooking. The combination has been part of the Cuban pantry for centuries.

Cuban picadillo is weekday food: quick, satisfying, and flexible. In Cuba it goes with white rice and sometimes black beans. It also works as a filling for fried empanadas and stuffed potatoes. The same mix of olives, raisins, and capers turns up across Latin American cooking that grew from Spanish colonial kitchens. The Mexican picadillo uses it. So do Puerto Rican sofrito dishes. In Cuba, this balance of sweet, salty, and savoury has been on the table for generations.

Piccalilli is the British mustard pickle. It mixes cauliflower florets, courgette, green beans, and shallots into a yellow turmeric sauce stiff with English mustard. Three weeks in the cupboard mellows the bite. The result is sweet-sharp and slightly hot. It goes with cold ham, pork pies, ploughman's lunches, and Boxing Day leftovers.

Pour the rest of the {{malt-vinegar}} of malt vinegar into a large preserving pan. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar, {{mustard-seeds-yellow}} of yellow mustard seeds, and {{coriander-seeds}} of crushed coriander seeds. Bring to a simmer over medium heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves.

Piccalilli is what late-Victorian British cookery called Indian pickle. The Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton printed it under that name in 1861. The word piccalilli took over by the end of the 1800s. The yellow colour and the mustard heat point to its roots in the Anglo-Indian kitchens of the East India Company. British cooks adapted Indian achar recipes to the vegetables and vinegars they had at home.

The aglione of the Val di Chiana is a large-bulbed, mild garlic. It is sweeter and less pungent than the standard supermarket type. It grows in the clay soils south of Arezzo. In the sauce named after it, aglione is used in amounts that would be too much with ordinary garlic. Four or five large cloves go in per portion. They cook slowly in olive oil until they are soft and almost melted, before the tomatoes go in. The result is deeply garlicky without being sharp.

All'aglione is the local dish of the Val di Chiana and the Senese countryside. Pici, the thick, hand-rolled pasta made from water and hard wheat flour, is the local pasta of choice. The sauce and the pasta belong together in Sienese cooking: pici all'aglione, pici all'amatriciana, pici al cinghiale. Outside Tuscany the dish is less known than carbonara or amatriciana. Within the region it is as canonical as anything on the pasta roster.
Add pasta water in small splashes, not all at once. Add the pecorino in three goes. Stir hard after each.

Pici is the pasta of the Sienese countryside: Montalcino, Pienza, Montepulciano, and the Crete Senesi. The shape is a very thick, irregular spaghetti, rolled by hand against a wooden board. It is uneven in places, with a dense chew that no machine-extruded pasta quite matches. Putting the Roman cacio e pepe method on a Tuscan pasta is a natural crossover. Both styles prize the same thing. They want starch, fat, and pepper in balance, with nothing else getting in the way. The result is heavier and more satisfying than the Roman original. The thicker noodle carries more starch, so the sauce binds more easily.

Pide culture in Turkey is concentrated on the Black Sea coast. Cities like Trabzon, Samsun, and Rize each have their own takes on shape, topping, and the type of cheese or meat used. The boat shape, called kayık pide, comes from the Trabzon region. Kıymalı (minced meat) pide is found across Turkey. It is one of the most common types in pide shops, called pidecis. A pideci is the Turkish answer to a pizzeria. It serves one thing: pide, in a rotating set of toppings, baked to order in a stone oven. The home version comes close, especially if you preheat the baking surface until it is as hot as your oven will manage.
Cool the filling all the way before you assemble. If the potatoes are very wet, spread them on a tray to dry a little before you mix in the cheese.

Pierogi are the national dish of Poland, or close enough that no other dish really competes for the title. The fillings shift by region and season: sauerkraut and mushroom, meat and onion, sweet cherry, blueberry. The ruskie filling is named not after Russia but after Ruthenia. That is the old borderland region of eastern Poland and western Ukraine where this potato and dairy filling grew up. Pierogi ruskie became the most popular type in modern Poland. Potatoes arrived later than the older grain and meat fillings. And the combination is very good. The boil-and-fry method shows up in 19th-century Polish household cookery guides as standard.

Pierogi are made on Christmas Eve and at Easter, at name days, at family gatherings, and on any weekday when the mood calls for comfort food. The fillings vary by region. Potato and cheese in Ruthenia and eastern Poland. Sauerkraut and mushroom in the west. Sweet curd cheese and fruit in the south. The potato version, pierogi ruskie, is the most common one. It is the one that Polish diaspora communities around the world tend to carry with them. Polish immigrants brought them to the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom in the 20th century. They are now so familiar in those places that people just call them Polish dumplings.
